classic.title: Classic
classic.rule: Place each digit exactly once in every row, column, and box.
classic.usage: Click a cell, then type a digit. Use Tab to cycle through available input tools.

odd even.title: Odd / Even
odd even.rule: A circle contains an odd digit; a square contains an even digit.
odd even.usage: Click a cell center to cycle through an odd circle, an even square, and no mark.

alloddalleven.title: All Odd All Even
alloddalleven.rule: Within each Sudoku box, all shaded cells contain digits of the same parity.
alloddalleven.usage: Use the Cell tool to shade every constrained cell. Shaded cells are grouped by their Sudoku box.

clone.title: Clone
clone.rule: Matching translated caged shapes contain identical digits in corresponding positions.
clone.usage: Draw cages around matching shapes. Every cage must have at least one other cage with exactly the same shape.

diagonal.title: Diagonal Sudoku
diagonal.rule: Each marked main diagonal contains every digit exactly once.
diagonal.usage: No mark is required. Select this button to inspect or activate the rule.

anti diagonal.title: Anti-Diagonal
anti diagonal.rule: Each marked diagonal contains exactly three distinct digits, each appearing three times.
anti diagonal.usage: No mark is required. Select this button to inspect or activate the rule.

anti king.title: Anti King (No touch)
anti king.rule: Equal digits may not be a chess king move apart.
anti king.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

anti knight.title: Anti-Knight
anti knight.rule: Equal digits may not be a chess knight move apart.
anti knight.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

non consecutive.title: Non-Consecutive
non consecutive.rule: Orthogonally adjacent cells may not contain consecutive digits.
non consecutive.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

arrow.title: Arrow
arrow.rule: Digits along an arrow sum to the digit in its circle.
arrow.usage: Drag from the circle cell through the shaft cells to create an arrow.

thermo.title: Thermometer
thermo.rule: Digits strictly increase from the bulb to the tip.
thermo.usage: Drag from the bulb through the cells in increasing order.

killer.title: Killer
killer.rule: Digits in a cage do not repeat and sum to the displayed cage total.
killer.usage: Drag across cells to make a cage, then use Killer Sum on its upper-left corner.

kropki.title: Kropki
kropki.rule: White dots join consecutive digits; black dots join digits in a 1:2 ratio.
kropki.usage: Click an edge to cycle through a black dot, a white dot, and no dot.

palindrome.title: Palindrome
palindrome.rule: Digits along the line read the same in either direction.
palindrome.usage: Drag through cell centers to draw a palindrome line.

xv.title: XV
xv.rule: Cells joined by V sum to 5; cells joined by X sum to 10.
xv.usage: Click an edge to cycle through V, X, and no clue.

battenburg.title: Battenburg
battenburg.rule: The four cells around a mark alternate odd and even like a checkerboard. Negative Battenburg forbids that pattern at every unmarked corner.
battenburg.usage: Click a four-cell corner to add or remove a Battenburg mark; use its Negative toggle to constrain unmarked corners.

skyscraper.title: Skyscraper
skyscraper.rule: An outside clue gives the number of visible digits when taller digits hide shorter ones.
skyscraper.usage: Click an outside clue cell and type its visibility count.

sandwich.title: Sandwich
sandwich.rule: An outside clue gives the sum of digits strictly between 1 and 6 in that row or column.
sandwich.usage: Click an outside clue cell and type the sandwich sum.

bouncing x-sums.title: Bouncing X-Sums
bouncing x-sums.rule: Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X cells along the indicated diagonal, starting from that cell, where X is the first digit placed in that direction. If the diagonal hits the edge, it bounces at a 90 degree angle.
bouncing x-sums.usage: Place the total and its arrow on the same outside cell.

czech outsider.title: Czech Outsider
czech outsider.rule: Each number outside the grid must appear on the diagonal indicated by its corresponding arrow. Multiple instances must appear at least that many times.
czech outsider.usage: Place the digits and its arrow on the same outside cell.

diagonal sum is nine.title: Diagonal Sum is Nine
diagonal sum is nine.rule: Pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to nine are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to nine.
diagonal sum is nine.usage: Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

diagonal tens.title: Diagonal Tens
diagonal tens.rule: Pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to ten are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to ten.
diagonal tens.usage: Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

disparity.title: Disparity
disparity.rule: Any two cells that share a border between two different boxes must contain digits of opposite parity.
disparity.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

distances.title: Distances
distances.rule: Clues outside the grid indicate the distance (in steps) between the digits in the corresponding row or column. Digits must be placed in order of appearance.
distances.usage: Click an outside clue cell and type its X-Y:Z clue (e.g. 2-5:4).

faded kropki.title: Faded Kropki
faded kropki.rule: All dots are white and indicate that the digits must be consecutive or have a 1:2 ratio. Undotted orthogonal pairs cannot be consecutive or have a 1:2 ratio.
faded kropki.usage: Place white dots between adjacent cells. Negative constraint can be toggled using the Negative Kropki button.

first seen odd/even.title: First Seen Odd/Even
first seen odd/even.rule: Clues outside the grid indicate the first digit of a specific parity (odd/even) seen from that direction.
first seen odd/even.usage: Click an outside cell and type the digit.

max ascending.title: Max Ascending
max ascending.rule: Clues outside indicate the length of the longest contiguous run of strictly ascending digits in that row/column from that direction.
max ascending.usage: Click an outside cell and type the digit.

fives.title: Fives
fives.rule: Adjacent cells separated by a diamond must sum to 5 or differ by 5. All possible diamonds are given.
fives.usage: Place diamond marks between adjacent cells.

frame-diagonal.title: Frame-Diagonal
frame-diagonal.rule: Clues outside indicate the sum of the first 3 cells along the diagonal in that direction.
frame-diagonal.usage: Place diagonal arrow symbols outside the grid, and enter the sum digits.

odd labyrinth.title: Odd Labyrinth
odd labyrinth.rule: There must be a continuous path of odd digits connecting r1c1 to r9c9.
odd labyrinth.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

even passage.title: Even Passage
even passage.rule: There must be a continuous path of even digits connecting r1c1 to r9c9.
even passage.usage: This is a global rule and requires no additional marks.

equal sum line.title: Equal Sum Line
equal sum line.rule: Digits on line segments within each 3x3 box must sum to the same total.

german whispers.title: German Whispers
german whispers.rule: Adjacent digits along the line must differ by at least 5.

factor lines.title: Factor Lines
factor lines.rule: Adjacent digits along the line must be factors or multiples of each other.

inner frame sum.title: Inner Frame Sum
inner frame sum.rule: Outside clues indicate the sum of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th cells in that row or column in that direction.
inner frame sum.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

missing digit.title: Missing Digit
missing digit.rule: Outside clues indicate the digit that is missing from the first three cells in that row or column in that direction.
missing digit.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

next to 9.title: Next to 9
next to 9.rule: Outside clues indicate the sum of the immediate neighbors of the digit 9 in that row or column.
next to 9.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

outside consecutive.title: Outside Consecutive
outside consecutive.rule: Outside clues indicate the count of consecutive adjacent pairs in that row or column.
outside consecutive.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

outside greater than.title: Outside Greater Than
outside greater than.rule: Outside clues indicate the count of neighboring cells in that row or column that contain a digit greater than their neighbor in that direction.
outside greater than.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

outside killer.title: Outside Killer
outside killer.rule: Outside clues indicate the sum of at least one pair of neighboring cells in that row or column.
outside killer.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

parity skyscrapers.title: Parity Sky Scrapers
parity skyscrapers.rule: Outside clues indicate whether an odd or even number of skyscrapers are visible in that direction.
parity skyscrapers.usage: Place number clues outside the grid.

pointing differents.title: Pointing Differents
pointing differents.rule: Outside clues indicate the count of distinct digits along the diagonal starting in that direction.
pointing differents.usage: Place diagonal arrow symbols outside the grid, and enter the count digits.

renbanline.title: Renban (line)
renbanline.rule: Digits along each line must form a set of consecutive digits in any order.

renbanregion.title: Renban (region)
renbanregion.rule: Digits in each marked region must form a set of consecutive digits in any order.

rules.9x9:
Apply classic sudoku rules.
Numbers placed along the marked line and circles must no repeat. The same set of 8 numbers is used along the circles.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, the range of digits is now 0-9. Some cells have been split with a diagonal, and two digits go into those cells.

Place a digit from 0-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and 3x3 box contains each digit exactly once.
Each digit placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the digits placed in the cells that the adjoining arrow passes through. Digits may repeat on arrows.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
Each number from 1-8 represents the height of the building. The clues outside the grid indicate the number of buildings visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.
0 does not represent a building, and does not count towards the number of buildings in a corresponding direction.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
The three numbers placed along marked lines must result in 24 after applying two of the arithmetic operations (+, x, -, /). The order of these numbers along the line does not matter.
E.g. 395 can be combined as 24 = (3 x 5) + 9.

Place a digit from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and outlined regions contains each digit exactly once.
This puzzle has 12 lines (instead of rows and columns) and 6 regions. The lines are not straight. The way to identify a line is by taking a cell at the edge of the grid and to follow a path by finding the next cell that touches the opposite side of the cell. You repeat this process until you reach the other edge.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. In each 3x3 outlined box, all shaded cells must contain digits of the same parity.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. In each 3x3 outlined box, all shaded cells must contain digits of the same parity.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Numbers formed by the digits on the gray lines are "almost palindromes", i.e. they can be read equally in both directions except for exactly one couple of cells that do not match on each line.

rules.6x6: Place an alphabet from the given set into each empty cell in the grid so that each alphabet appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box.
rules.9x9: Place an alphabet from the given set into each empty cell in the grid so that each alphabet appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box.

rules.6x6: Along each line, digits alternate between being larger and smaller than their immediate neighbours. Digits do not repeat on a line.
rules.9x9: Along each line, digits alternate between being larger and smaller than their immediate neighbours. Digits do not repeat on a line.

instructions: Draw every alternating stripe as a grey diagonal-style line.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each main diagonal contains exactly three digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each main diagonal contains exactly three digits.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. No cell that is a knight-step away can contain the same digit. A knight's move is 2 in a line and 1 to the side, as in chess.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. No cell that is a knight-step away can contain the same digit. A knight's move is 2 in a line and 1 to the side, as in chess.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Adjacent cells marked with X cannot contain digits which are consecutive to each other. Not all such adjacent cells are marked. In other words, adjacent cells with no marking may or may not contain consecutive digits.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum or difference or product or ratio of digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum or difference or product or ratio of digits in those two cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digit in each circled cell is the sum of digits along the path of its arrow. Digits can repeat within an arrow shape.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digit in each circled cell is the sum of digits along the path of its arrow. Digits can repeat within an arrow shape.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, there are circles and arrows in the grid. For a digit N in a circle, the digit N+1 is at the end of the attached arrow. The digits along the arrow (not including the circle) must form a sequence of consecutive digits. (The digit in the circle can appear along the arrow itself.)

Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first set of all ascending digits, starting from the first cell from the corresponding side. Sets may consist of only one digit.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. If the digit in a cell is the average of its two horizontal neighbours then the cell is marked with a horizontal bar. If the digit in a cell is the average of its two vertical neighbours then the cell is marked with a vertical bar. All possible bars are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. If the digit in a cell is the average of its two horizontal neighbours then the cell is marked with a horizontal bar. If the digit in a cell is the average of its two vertical neighbours then the cell is marked with a vertical bar. All possible bars are marked.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The number placed in a cell with a circle must be the arithmetic mean of the digits along the path of each arrow. Numbers may repeat on an arrow.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each 2x2 area with two odd digits and two even digits forming a checkerboard pattern is marked with a battenburg symbol (or circle). All such 2x2 areas are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each 2x2 area with two odd digits and two even digits forming a checkerboard pattern is marked with a battenburg symbol (or circle). All such 2x2 areas are marked.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Wherever 2 odd and 2 even digits form a 2x2 chessboard pattern, a Battenburg marking is given. If there is no marking, the above pattern is not allowed. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digits contained in the cells in the direction of the corresponding arrow.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each ship outside the grid must appear in the grid such that ships do not touch each other, even diagonally. Rotation and reflection are allowed but the orienta ion of digits in the ships remain the same. Ship segments without a digit can contain any digit. Each number outside the grid is the number of ship segments in the corresponding row or column. Cells with given digits or a water symbol are not part of any ship.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each ship outside the grid must appear in the grid such that ships do not touch each other, even diagonally. Rotation and reflection are allowed but the orientation of digits in the ships remain the same. Ship segments without a digit can contain any digit. Each number outside the grid is the number of ship segments in the corresponding row or column. Cells with given digits or a water symbol are not part of any ship.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, place the given set of battleships into the grid; ships cannot touch even diagonally. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digits occupied by ships in the corresponding row or column.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, numbers outside the grid indicate the sum of all digits before the digit 1 in that row/column or after the digit 9 in that row/column, going from left to right and from top to bottom.
Apply Classic Sudoku rules. Additionally, the clues outside the grid show the sum of the digits that appear before 9 in the corresponding row or column.

rules.6x6: Every interior digit on a line lies strictly between the digits in the circles at the two ends. Digits may repeat on a line.
rules.9x9: Every interior digit on a line lies strictly between the digits in the circles at the two ends. Digits may repeat on a line.

instructions: Draw the line and place a white circle on each endpoint.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Each of the grey twisty bands must contain one of the given 7-digit numbers. The numbers can be entered in either of two directions.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the sums of groups in the given order. Clues on top refer to groups of big digits (5-9). These groups are separated by at least one small digit (1-4). Clues on the left refer to groups of small digits, that are separated by at least one big digit.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the largest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the largest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.

rules.6x6: Each inequality sign between boxes compares the sums of the corresponding rows or columns on its two sides.
rules.9x9: Each inequality sign between boxes compares the sums of the corresponding rows or columns on its two sides.

instructions: Place <, ^, >, or v between the two block slices being compared.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X cells along the indicated diagonal, starting from that cell, where X is the first digit placed in that direction. If the diagonal hits the edge, it bounces at a 90 degree angle. Digits may be repeated in the sums.

instructions: Place the total and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. If two neighbouring cells are connected with a grey line, they contain consecutive digits. All possible sequences with length of at least 3 are marked inside each 3x3 box.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The dots given inside some cells form part (or all) of the Braille representation of the digit.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid indicates the first cell where the sum of digits in the corresponding direction is greater than 21.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Place a number from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked region each number exactly once. Note that cells containing capsules span three columns.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Symbols around the grid describe in correct order all increasing and decreasing sequences of length 3 or more that exist in the corresponding row/column. Sharp end of each symbol points where the small numbers are, open end is where the big numbers are. Number inside a symbol gives the length of a sequence.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Two numbers are white and black chess Kings. As in real chess, Kings can not touch other Kings, not even diagonally, even if they are not in opposite color. It is a part of solving to find which numbers are chess Kings.

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x2 outlined box.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x4 outlined box.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each 1x4 marked area represents a digital clock that displays a valid time from left-to-right in 24-hour (HH:MM) format.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each 1x4 marked area represents a digital clock that displays a valid time from left-to-right in 24-hour (HH:MM) format.

instructions: Draw a cage around each horizontal group of four clock digits; other cage shapes are ignored.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 0-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 3x3 box contains each digit exactly once. Some digital clocks are shown. When the puzzle is completed, all clocks should display a valid time in 24-hour format (HH:MM); i.e. a clock cannot display 43:65.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in clockwise direction is marked with a white circle at the intersection. Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in anti-clockwise direction is marked with a black circle at the intersection. All such circles are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in clockwise direction is marked with a white circle at the intersection. Each 2x2 area with digits increasing in anti-clockwise direction is marked with a black circle at the intersection. All such circles are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be identical.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be identical.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits in the same place in both shaded figures must be identical. A digit placed in a shaded cell must be strictly greater than digits placed in orthogonally adjacent unshaded cells.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each shape in the grid appears four times. Two shapes are clones, and digits in the same place in each shape must be identical. Other two shapes are renban groups, and they must contain a set of distinct, consecutive digits in any order. Cloned shapes may or may not be renban groups at the same time, and digits in it may repeat.
Identifying which shapes are clones and which are renban groups is part of the solving.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each shape is cloned, including the digits it contains, without rotating or mirroring. The position of the digits inside each shape is fixed - the same digits have to be placed at the same position inside the identical shapes. The digit may appear more than once in a shape.

rules.6x6: Every marked strand has exactly the same sequence of digits. A digit may repeat on a strand.
rules.9x9: Every marked strand has exactly the same sequence of digits. A digit may repeat on a strand.

instructions: Draw the equal strands as diagonal grey lines of the same length.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Some digits have been substituted by letters. All instances of the same letter must be substituted by the same digit and different letters must be substituted by different digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Some digits have been substituted by letters. All instances of the same letter must be substituted by the same digit and different letters must be substituted by different digits.

instructions: Enter the code letter at the upper-left corner of its cell.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Two cages with a dashed border are marked by the same letter if they contain the same set of digits. For each cage marked with the same letter digits in the same place must be identical. Digits may repeat in a cage.

rules.6x6: Two-cell cages marked with the same letter contain the same pair of digits in either order.
rules.9x9: Two-cell cages marked with the same letter contain the same pair of digits in either order.

instructions: Cage each pair and give matching pairs the same code.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. The clues with an arrow outside the grid indicate the number of even digits in the indicated direction. However, some of these clues are replaced by letters. Different letters mean different numbers. Lettered clues can be equal to any of the given numbered clues.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a circle (or bar) contain consecutive digits. All possible circles (or bars) are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a circle (or bar) contain consecutive digits. All possible circles (or bars) are marked.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits in the grey areas must form a chain of consecutive digits from 1 to 9 by moving horizontally or vertically from one cell to the next.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be consecutive.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits in the same corresponding cell in each shaded figure must be consecutive.

Apply classic sudoku rules. If a circle is given between two adjacent cells, then the digits in those cells must be consecutive. Not all circles are given; adjacent cells without a circle may contain either consecutive numbers or non-consecutive numbers.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each white circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there is exactly one consecutive pair of digits in those four cells. Each black circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there are at least two consecutive pairs of digits in those four cells. Different pairs can use the same digit. Not all possible circles are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each white circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there is exactly one consecutive pair of digits in those four cells. Each black circle at the intersection of four cells indicates that there are at least two consecutive pairs of digits in those four cells. Different pairs can use the same digit. Not all possible circles are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit assigned to 'C' is in a corner cell of the 3x3 outlined box pointed by its arrow. Each digit assigned to 'E' is in an edge cell of the 3x3 outlined box pointed by its arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit assigned to 'C' is in a corner cell of the 3x3 outlined box pointed by its arrow. Each digit assigned to 'E' is in an edge cell of the 3x3 outlined box pointed by its arrow.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle tells how many distinct digits you can find along the corresponding arrow.
Number in a circle tells the count of odd digits in up to 10 cells touching the pair of cells with the circle.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle tells you how many odd digits you can find along the corresponding arrow.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a cell with a circle tells you how many distinct digits you can find in up to 8 neighbouring cells (sharing edge or corner). Number in a cell with a cross tells you how many distinct digits you can find in up to 4 diagonally neighbouring cells (sharing a corner). All possible circles and crosses are drawn.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are monotonically increasing or decreasing.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are monotonically increasing or decreasing.

instructions: Draw each monotonic line with the no-bulb thermo tool.

rules.6x6: At every marked four-cell intersection, the two diagonal pairs have equal sums. All such intersections are marked.
rules.9x9: At every marked four-cell intersection, the two diagonal pairs have equal sums. All such intersections are marked.

instructions: Place a cross at each four-cell intersection.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Place all the listed number sequences into shaded strips. Number sequences can be entered in any of four directions.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid must appear on the diagonal indicated by its corresponding arrow. If a number is given multiple times for a single arrow, that number must appear at least as many times as it is specified on that diagonal.

instructions: Place the digits and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. There are some white and grey arrows in the grid. The digit placed in the cell with white arrow must not repeat in the direction of the arrow. The digit placed in the cell with grey arrow is repeated at least once in the direction of the arrow.

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. No digit can repeat in any outlined region.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Kropki dots are placed in the grid. Kropki rules are applied only for a pair of cells where the centers of cells are connected (orthogonally or diagonally) with a grey line.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. For each pair of digits (X and Y) outside the grid, either X is in the Yth position in the corresponding direction or Y is in the Xth position in the corresponding direction or both.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. For each pair of digits (X and Y) outside the grid, either X is in the Yth position in the corresponding direction or Y is in the Xth position in the corresponding direction or both.

instructions: Enter a two-digit XY clue in the outside margin.

rules.6x6: A diagonal arrow in a cell containing N points toward another N on that diagonal sightline. All possible arrows are drawn.
rules.9x9: A diagonal arrow in a cell containing N points toward another N on that diagonal sightline. All possible arrows are drawn.

instructions: Place only the diagonal arrows that detect the origin digit.

rules.6x6: A circled clue at a four-cell intersection gives a×d−b×c for the surrounding 2×2 matrix.
rules.9x9: A circled clue at a four-cell intersection gives a×d−b×c for the surrounding 2×2 matrix.

instructions: Enter the determinant at the four-cell intersection.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each main diagonal contains each digit from 1 to 6.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each main diagonal contains each digit from 1 to 9.
Apply classic sudoku rules. One marked diagonal must contains digits from 1-9 exactly once and the other contains exactly 3 different digits.

Place a digit from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 9-cell region contains each digit exactly once. Each marked diagonal must also contain each digit from 1-9 exactly once.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are exactly 13 pairs of cells in the grid (15 in example) satisfying the following conditions: one cell is grey, one cell is white, both contain the same digit, which is equal to the diagonal distance between the cells. There is no such pair with both cells white or both cells grey. Each digit from 1 to 8 appears at least once in a grey cell. Each cell / digit belongs to at most one pair.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each number represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate the number of buildings visible from the corresponding direction. A higher building will hide any lower buildings behind it. Each marked diagonal must also contain each number from 1-9 exactly once.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. All pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to nine are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to nine.

instructions: Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. All pairs of diagonally adjacent cells that contain digits summing to ten are marked with a line; diagonally adjacent cells without a line cannot sum to ten.

instructions: Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Diagonally touching cells that contain consecutive digits are marked by a bar. All possible bars are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Diagonally touching cells that contain consecutive digits are marked by a bar. All possible bars are marked.

instructions: Toggle the left and right diagonal bars at each intersection.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits in diagonally touching cells must not be consecutive.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits in diagonally touching cells must not be consecutive.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the difference of digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the difference of digits in those two cells.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each digit in a grey cell must have at least one orthogonally adjacent digit (in eather a grey or a white cell) that is exactly 2 greater or smaller.
There may also be digits in white cells which have orthohonally adjacent digits that are exactly 2 greater or smaller.

instructions: Shade each cell subject to the constraint.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. The small number in a two-cell cage indicates how many different digits surround the cage. Up to 10 cells touching the cage by a side or by a corner should be inspected.

Apply classic sudoku rules. For each grey line, there must be at least one way of removing a digit so that the remaining digits on the line form a palindrome. It is permissible that the digits on the grey line also form a palindrom before the removal step.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. No digit can appear in the same cell position in different 2x3 outlined boxes.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. No digit can appear in the same cell position in different 3x3 outlined boxes.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Any two cells that share a border between two different 3x3 boxes must contain digits of opposite parity.

Nine grey cells are uniquely paired to nine occurances of digit 5, the distance (vertical, horizontal or diagonal) from grey cell to digit 5 is given in the grey cell. Digit 5 cannot be placed in a grey cell.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Outside clues are of the form 'X-Y:Z', which indicates that digit X comes before digit Y (from left-to-right for rows, or top-to-bottom for columns) and the distance between them is exactly Z cells (where Z=1 means X and Y are next to each other).

instructions: Click an outside clue cell and type its X-Y:Z clue (e.g. 2-5:3).

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Inside each 3x3 box all sums created by three horizontally or vertically adjacent digits must be divisible by 3.

rules.6x6: A circled edge clue divides the two-digit number formed by its adjacent cells, read left-to-right or top-to-bottom.
rules.9x9: A circled edge clue divides the two-digit number formed by its adjacent cells, read left-to-right or top-to-bottom.

instructions: Enter the divisor between the two adjacent cells.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1-4 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly twice in each row, column, and outlined region. Identical digits cannot share an edge.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Adjacent cells contianing numbers whose difference is 2 are marked with a white rhombus. Adjacent cells containing numbers whose ratio is 4 are marked with a black rhombus. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain numbers either whose difference is 2 or whose ratio is 4.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digit(s) placed between the digits 1, 5 and 9 in the respective row or column in the correct order. The order of the digits 1,5 and 9 is not given.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
Each 9 in the grid sees exactly 8 other cells in the 4 vertical and horizontal directions before a wall is seen. Numbers placed in each of these sets of 8 cells must not repeat.

Place a digit from the given set into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The remaining cells are blank. Each digit outside the grid is the first digit seen from the corresponding direction.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the difference of digits in the first and the last cell in the corresponding direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the difference of digits in the first and the last cell in the corresponding direction.

rules.6x6: A circled digit between two cells appears in one of those two cells.
rules.9x9: A circled digit between two cells appears in one of those two cells.

instructions: Enter the value that must occur in either adjacent cell.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit in a cell with an arrow does not appear in any of the cells pointed by its arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit in a cell with an arrow does not appear in any of the cells pointed by its arrow.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Several cells are marked with a star. In all four directions, maximal possible length is given until the sum of the digits is higher than the value of an emitter.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits subtract to the same absolute difference. All possible crosses are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits subtract to the same absolute difference. All possible crosses are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits multiply to the same product. All possible crosses are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits multiply to the same product. All possible crosses are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits divide to the same ratios. All possible crosses are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits divide to the same ratios. All possible crosses are marked.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The line is divided by the borders of the 3x3 boxes. The digits on a line segment within any given 3x3 box must sum to the same total as the digits on every other segment of that same line.

instructions: Draw the lines of the same length.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. All lines have the same sum of digits. Digits may repeat on lines.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits add to the same sum. All possible crosses are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each cross at the intersection of four cells indicates that the two pairs of diagonally opposite digits add to the same sum. All possible crosses are marked.

Apply classic Sudoku rules. Each of the shaded cells has an escape path to the edge of the grid. An escape path consists of a series of adjacent cells containing a descending consecutive sequence of digits. All escape paths begin with the digit in the shaded cell and end at a cell containing a 1 at the edge of the grid. Escape paths starting in different shaded cells never share any cell. It is possible that a shaded cell can have more than one escape path.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded squares contain even digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded squares contain even digits.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. There must be at least one continuous path of cells containing only even digits that travels orthogonally from the top-left corner (r1c1) to the bottom-right corner (r9c9).

instructions: The constraint is generated from grid geometry.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two even digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two even digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.

instructions: Enter every sandwiched digit in one of the three top or left margin layers; leave all three blank to require no sandwiched digit.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is even. All possible circles are not marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is even. All possible circles are not marked.

instructions: Place one white circle on the shared edge of each marked pair.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit at the intersection of cells must not be present in any of those cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit at the intersection of cells must not be present in any of those cells.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The rows and columns continue over an empty space.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first X digits placed in the corresponding direction, where X is equal to the first digit placed in that direction. There are different letters and mathematical expressions (consisting of only +, -, x and /) inside and outside the grid. Each letter represents a positive integer. Different letters may take on the same value.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each marked 10-cell string must contain all digits from 1 to 9. Digits at both ends of each string must be the same.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each marked region must contain each digit from 1-9 exactly twice.

instructions: Shade each extra large region as one orthogonally connected group of cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each marked region contains each digit from 1 to 6.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each marked region contains each digit from 1 to 9.

instructions: Shade each extra region as one orthogonally connected group of cells.

rules.9x9: Place a number from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked box contains each number exactly once. The grey cells will not contain numbers.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. For any two adjacent digits on the line, one digit must be a factor or a multiple of the other.

instructions: Draw the lines of the same length.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. A white dot between two adjacent cells indicates that the digits in those cells must either be consecutive (differ by 1) or have a 1:2 ratio. All possible white dots are given: cells without a dot cannot be consecutive or have a 1:2 ratio.

instructions: Place white dots between adjacent cells.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Place the given set of figures into the grid following grid lines. Figures can be rotated, but not reflected. Figures cannot touch each other even in a point. Clues show the sum of neighboring digits which are inside the figures.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Dotted areas with the same shape must contain the same set of digits (but not necessarily in the same order). The digit may be repeated in area.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The digit in each shaded cell is greater than the digits in the first cell of the corresponding row (leftmost cell) and the first cell of the corresponding column (topmost cell). Not all possible cells are shaded.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Clues outside the grid indicate the first digit of a specific parity (odd or even) encountered when looking into the grid from that direction. Any number of opposite-parity digits may appear before the first seen digit.

instructions: Enter the parity clue digit in the margin.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Two cells separated by a diamond must contain digits that have a sum of 5 or a difference of 5. All possible diamonds are given: cells without a diamond cannot have a sum or difference of 5.

instructions: Place diamond marks between adjacent cells.

rules.9x9:
Fill in the grid with digits 1 to 9 so that every row, column and outlined box contains nine different digits.
There are two grids to be solved on two sides of the paper. There are 12 extra squares along the grid edge. You have to bend each of those extra squares (independently) to the one or the other side to form two classic sudokus of the standard size. You should solve them after that.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Grey circles represent 14 football players divided into two teams. Players of each team are marked with numbers from 1 to 7. One team plays with a ball number 8, the other with a ball number 9. Player number 1 has a ball number next to him in a direction of a horizontal, vertical or diagonal pass to the player number 2. And so on up to the pass between players 6 and 7 of each team. No other player stands in the way of every pass.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. If a shaded cell and an unshaded cell are adjacent then the digit in the shaded cell is larger.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. If a shaded cell and an unshaded cell are adjacent then the digit in the shaded cell is larger.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are two independent groups of eight cells in the grid, marked with a grey shading. Exactly four different digits can be found in each of the groups, each of them exactly twice.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid are equal to the sum of the first 3 digits appearing in the corresponding row or column.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first 3 cells along the indicated diagonal.

instructions: Place diagonal arrow outside and enter its sum.

rules.9x9:
Apply classic sudoku rules.
A circle marked at the intersection of a 2×2 block of cells indicates that numbers placed in these cells are either all odd or all even. A square marked at the intersection of a 2×2 block of cells indicates that exactly two numbers placed in these cells are odd, and exactly two numbers are even.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each row and column forms two n-digit numbers, where n is the grid size, read in opposite directions. Each number outside the grid indicates the rank of the corresponding n-digit number, starting from the smallest.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each row and column forms two n-digit numbers, where n is the grid size, read in opposite directions. Each number outside the grid indicates the rank of the corresponding n-digit number, starting from the smallest.

Place a number from 1 to X (integers only) into each cell so that each number appears exactly once in each row and column. (X is the number of cells in each row.) Some inequality relations are given between adjacent cells; they must apply to the numbers in those cells.
Numbers may not repeat in any of the outlined regions.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Exactly one circle should be added on each grey line. One or two arrows should be drawn at all loose ends of each grey line then. Finally solve standard arrows sudoku. A number placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the numbers placed in cells the adjoining arrow passes through. Numbers may repeat on arrows.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent digits along the line must differ by at least 5.

instructions: Draw the lines of the same length.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the larger of the digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the larger of the digits in those two cells.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each number between two neighbouring cells is either the greater of the digits or the difference between the digits in those two cells.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each number between two neighbouring cells is either the greater of the digits or the sum of the digits in those two cells.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There are several cages in the grid with a dashed-line outline. Sum of all digits in every cage is given.
Same digit cannot be repeated in one cage. All digits should follow given inequality signs.
Apply classic sudoku rules. All equality and inequality signs relate to the sums of killer cages. Some of the cages have no sum value attached. Digits must not repeat in cages.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number at the intersection of cells is the sum of digits in those cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number at the intersection of cells is the sum of digits in those cells.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
Adjacent cells containing consecutive numbers are marked with inequalities. Numbers placed in these cells must satisfy the given inequality. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain consecutive numbers.
Apply classic sudoku rules. At some intersections of two crossing grid lines, a set of four digits is given. For each set, exactly 2 digits are correct (they appear at least once).

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells are divided in half and there must be a digit in any one half.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Two of the gray lines are perfect palindromes and the other two gray lines are consecutive pair palindromes (i.e. paired palindrome positions must have digits which are consecutive to each other). Part of solving is to figure out which lines are perfect palindromes and which are consecutive pair palindromes.

Fill in the grid with digits 1 to 9 so that every row, column and outlined box contains nine different digits. If there are two digits in one cell, the smaller one should be to the left from the higher one.

Place a digit from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked region contains each digit exactly once. Some cells are halved by one of the diagonals. For any such cell, exactly one of its two halves shall contain a digit. That digit is considered to belong to the row and column containing its cell, as well as to the region connected to the half cell the digit is in.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each of the four marked diagonals must also contain each digit from 1-9 exactly once.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined region. Digits do not repeat along any of the three directions in which the hexagonal cells share edges.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The shaded region must be at least once cloned somewhere else in the grid, without rotation or reflection and the digits in the corresponding cells must be equal. The clone may overlap partially with the original shaded region, but not completely. Digits may repeat in a shaded region.

Apply classic sudoku rules. There must be a path from 1 in the arrow to 9 in the target via the digits 2 to 8 so that 2 must be placed in a cell in the direction of the arrow. Consider the cell with 2 a new arrow cell but with the arrow turned 90 degrees to the right. Now 3 must be in a cell in that direction. Consider the cell with 3 a new arrow but with the arrow turned another 90 degrees to the right. Now 4 must be in a cell in that direction ... and so on until you hit the target.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit at the intersection of cells must be present in at least one of those cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit at the intersection of cells must be present in at least one of those cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each inequality sign (‘<’ or ‘>’) between adjacent cells indicates the larger of the two digits is on the open side of the sign.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each inequality sign (‘<’ or ‘>’) between adjacent cells indicates the larger of the two digits is on the open side of the sign.
Apply classic sudoku rules. For each pair of adjacent boxes, the inequality relationship between three pairs of numbers across the box boundary is the same.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid represents sum of 2nd, 3rd and 4th digit in the corresponding direction.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Grey cells contain the same digits as the corresponding grey cells in the other puzzle.

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.

Place a digit from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 9-cell region contains each digit exactly once. Along each grey stripe, digits must be alternately larger and smaller than their immediate neighbours. Digits may not repeat anywhere on a stripe.

Place a digit from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked contains each digit exactly once. Some region boundaries are missing, but all given borders must separate cells in different reqions.

Follow irrregular sudoku rules. Rows and columns are marked with numbers 1 to 9. There is a white dot between two cells if the difference of the digits they contain is equal to the number of a row they are part of. There is a black dot between two cells if the sum of the digits they contain is equal to the number of a row they are part of. The same applies to the columns. All possible dots are drawn.
Place a digit from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 9-cell region contains each digit exactly once. Some regions are connected by lines.

Place numbers from 1-8 in the grid so that each number appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region.
The numbers on the right and left show the sum of the first 2 numbers in the row from the given side. The numbers on top and bottom
show the product of the first 2 numbers in the column from the given side.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, as in Diagonal Sudoku, each digit appears exactly once in the two main diagonals; as in Killer sudoku, the sum of the digits in each cage must equal the value given in the upper-left corner of that cage. Digits cannot repeat inside a cage.
(Note: Some cages will have cells connected only diagonally.)

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Shade some cells in the grid such that the numbers outside the grid represent sums of digits in white cells of the corresponding row or column. If there is more than one sum, the given order is valid and there must be at least one shaded cell between the sums. Some shaded cells may be given.

Follow irregular sudoku rules. There are several cages in the grid with a dashed-line outline. (Several of them are formed by two or three diagonally connected cells.) Sum of all digits in every cage is given. Same digit cannot be repeated in one cage.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the sum of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the sum of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.

Fill in the grid with digits 0 to 7 so that every row, column and boldly outlined region contains two zeros and all remaining digits exactly once. Two cells with a zero cannot share an edge. No digit can be repeated in a region with a dashed border. The sum of all digits in every region with a dashed border is given.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits places in each marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left corner. Digits must not repeat in cages. All cages contain a standard arrow clue, which is hidden. The circle part of the arrow will be at one end of the cage with the arrow extending along the cage to the other end. The arrow must fill the entire cage. The circle may enclose any number of digits, and multi-digit totals are read in the direction of the arrow.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The digits placed in each marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left. Digits must not repeat in cages. Numbers along the arrows indicate the sum of the digits in the corresponding direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Two cells which are a knight's move apart must not contain digits which add to either 5 or 15.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a white circle contain consecutive digits. Adjacent cells marked with a black circle contains digits where one digit is double of the other digit. The circle between 1 and 2 can be of either colour. All possible circles are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a white circle contain consecutive digits. Adjacent cells marked with a black circle contains digits where one digit is double of the other digit. The circle between 1 and 2 can be of either colour. All possible circles are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a white circle contain consecutive digits. Adjacent cells marked with a black circle contains digits where one digit is double of the other digit. The circle between 1 and 2 can be of either colour. All possible circles are not marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked with a white circle contain consecutive digits. Adjacent cells marked with a black circle contains digits where one digit is double of the other digit. The circle between 1 and 2 can be of either colour. All possible circles are not marked.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Whenever two adjacent numbers in a row or column add up to 50 or 100, this is marked by a letter L or C respectively, placed on the line separating the two numbers. Two-digit numbers are formed by combining adjacent digits (reading from left to right or top to bottom only). All possible letters L and C are marked. Row and column strings which would result in the leters L and C coinciding (e.g. 5248) will not occur.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the smaller of the digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the smaller of the digits in those two cells.
Place a letter in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 3x3 box contains the same set of letters. There are no repeated letters in the set.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
Exactly one given clue in each row, column and marked 3 × 3 box is incorrect.

Apply classic sudoku rules. One diagonal must contain each digit from 1-9 exactly once, whilst the other diagonal must have one or more digits repeated. It is part of solving to determine which diagonal has the corresponding property.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The digits placed in all but one marked cage must sum to the total given in its top-left. Exactly one marked cage displays a wrong sum. Digits must not repeat in cages.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits in circles must be odd and digits in squares must be even except for exactly one cell which has the wrong symbol.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each clue in the top-left corner of a marked cage displays two digits correctly and one incorrectly. Digits may repeat in cages.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.

instructions: Place the total and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. There are 80 numbers in the grid that fulfill a following condition: each of these happy numbers has a consecutive friend in a cell sharing an edge with it. There is exactly one lonely number in the grid that doesn't have a consecutive friend next to it.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Three of the given 3x3 blocks are magic squares, which means that the sum of the digits on both diagonals, the three horizontal lines of three cells and the three vertical lines of three cells is the same.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The grey central box serves as an exemplar. The four corner boxes are compared to the central one and the result of comparison is given in the style of a Mastermind puzzle for every triplet of cells. A black dot is given for every identical digit in the same position. A white dot is given for an identical digit but not in the same position. A cross means that there is nothing in common in this triplet.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Some boxes contain four numbers between adjacent cells that are the result of a different mathematical operation (+, -, *, /) of the digits in those cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Some boxes contain four numbers between adjacent cells that are the result of a different mathematical operation (+, -, *, /) of the digits in those cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number and mathematical operation at the intersection of cells indicates that the number is the result of applying the mathematical operation to each pair of diagonally opposite cells of the intersection. Each 'O' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are odd. Each 'E' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are even. Some numbers may be replaced with '?'.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number and mathematical operation at the intersection of cells indicates that the number is the result of applying the mathematical operation to each pair of diagonally opposite cells of the intersection. Each 'O' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are odd. Each 'E' at the intersection of cells indicates that the digits in those cells are even. Some numbers may be replaced with '?'.
Apply Classic Sudoku rules. Also some areas are overlapped, with sizes from 6×6 to 9×9.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Clues outside the grid indicate the length of the longest contiguous run of strictly ascending digits in that row or column viewed from the direction of the clue.

instructions: Enter the ascending sequence length in the margin.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the difference between the highest and the lowest digit in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. In each set of cells connected by meandering diagonal lines each digit appears at most once.
