Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the median of the digits in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

Place mines into the empty cells in the grid, at most one mine per cell, so that each number in a cell represents the number of mines adjacent to that cell (including diagonally adjacent cells).
Each row, column, and outlined region in the grid must contain exactly 3 mines.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the highest and the lowest digit in the first three cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The four corner boxes contain the same digits in symmetric positions about the centre.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The four corner boxes contain the same digits in symmetric positions about the centre.
Place a digit from 0-9 in each empty cell. Digits in a row, column and 3x3 box must be different. The clues outside the grid indicate the digit that is not included in the corresponding row/column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The combination of odd and even digits along an arrow corresponds to the "Morse-like coding" for the digit in the circled cell which follows.
Place a number from 1-9 in each empty cell in the overlapping grids such that each row, column and marked 3×3 box contains each number exactly once.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. No digit can repeat in any of the marked diagonals.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The number in each circle (or oval), reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, is a multiple or a divisor of the other number it is connected to. The two numbers can be the same.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The number in each circle (or oval), reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, is a multiple or a divisor of the other number it is connected to. The two numbers can be the same.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Some numbers are given on the edges between two adjacent cells. Treating those cells as a two-digit number (reading left to right or top to bottom), the two-digit number must be a multiple of the clue. (For example, near a 7 clue, the two digits can be 14, 21, 28, 35,...)

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The number formed from left-to-right in the bottom row of each cage is the product of all the digits in the top row of the cage.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The number formed from left-to-right in the bottom row of each cage is the product of all the digits in the top row of the cage.

instructions: Draw each cage; multiplication signs may be placed manually on its internal edges.

rules.9x9:
Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, some edges are marked with the sums of the digits in the adjacent cells.
Apply Classic Sudoku rules. Additionally, clues outside the grid indicate the digits that sit next to 9 in that row or column. These clues are given in increasing order.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each line contains each digit from 1 to 9.
Apply class sudoku rules. For each number 1-9, there must be at least one set of three identical digits in a straight line of adjacent cells somewhere in the grid.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, no group of three contiguous cells within any row or column can contain a set of consecutive digits in any order. (For example, the grid cannot have 345, 354, 435, 453, 534, or 543 in three contiguous cells).

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Even digits must not be in adjacent cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Even digits must not be in adjacent cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits in any three consecutive cells in any row or column must not be of the same parity.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits in any three consecutive cells in any row or column must not be of the same parity.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits in adjacent cells must not be consecutive.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits in adjacent cells must not be consecutive.

Follow classic sudoku rules. (The classic sudoku regions are marked with the shading not by the bold lines.) Pair of adjacent cells following the spiral way marked by a bold line cannot contain consecutive digits.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits must not repeat in cages and their sums end with 5 (the sums can be 5, 15, 25, 35 or 45).

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid is the digit in the Xth cell in the corresponding direction where X is the digit in the first cell in that direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid is the digit in the Xth cell in the corresponding direction where X is the digit in the first cell in that direction.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded squares contain even digits. Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells with shaded squares contain even digits. Cells with shaded circles contain odd digits.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Sum of the two digits in marked cages is given. Every pair of blank cells (= cells outside marked cages) sharing an edge have different parity.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Some circled cells are connected by a bridge. An odd digit in a circle denotes the number of odd digits on the bridge. An even digit in a circle equals the number of even digits on the bridge. The digits on the circles are not counted. If a circled cell is connected by more than one bridge, an odd/even digit in the cell denote the number of odd/even digits on each connected bridge (not the sum of all connected bridges).

Apply classic sudoku rules. An even digit placed in a cell with a circle indicates the number of even digits placed in the surrounding 8 cells. An odd digit placed in a cell with a circle indicates the number of odd digits placed in the surrounding cells. Numbers placed in cells without a circle may have either of these properties.
Follow classic sudoku rules. Moreover, the sum of two digits in every cage marked with “O” is odd and the sum of two digits in every cage marked with “E” is even.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. There must be at least one continuous path of cells containing only odd digits that travels orthogonally from the top-left corner (r1c1) to the bottom-right corner (r9c9).

instructions: The constraint is generated from grid geometry.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two odd digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid indicates that the digit is sandwiched between two odd digits in its adjacent cells in the corresponding direction. All such digits are given.

instructions: Enter every sandwiched digit in one of the three top or left margin layers; leave all three blank to require no sandwiched digit.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is odd. All possible circles are not marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells marked by a circle contain digits whose sum is odd. All possible circles are not marked.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
The clues outside the grid indicate, in the correct order, the sums of all consecutive sequences of odd numbers in the corresponding direction. There must be at least one even number placed between two such sequences.
Apply classic sudoku rules. All odd digits must be orthogonally interconnected with no 2x2 area covered completely by odd digits (like shaded cells in Tapa puzzle).
Apply classic sudoku rules. Exactly one odd and one even digit must be placed in each marked pair of adjacent cells.

Place a digit from 1-8 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 8-cell box contains each number exactly once. The clues outside the grid indicate that the first two cells in the corresponding direction must contain digits matching the following description:
O = Odd digits {1, 3, 5, 7}; E = Even digits {2, 4, 6, 8}; B = Big digits {5, 6, 7, 8}; S = Small digits {1, 2, 3, 4}.
Apply classic sudoku rules. A digit in a grey cell must be placed in the Nth position in the next row, where N is the digit immediately to the right of the grey cell.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Some given digits are shaded. All such digits have exactly one digit of the same value one knight step away from its own position.

Apply classic sudoku rules. If a circle is given between two adjacent cells, then the difference in those cells must be 1 or 2. Not all circles are given; adjacent cells without a circle may contain either consecutive digits, digits whose difference is 2 or other non-consecutive digits.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Every digit 1-9 has exactly one touching pair in the grid. All these touches are marked by a circle. If there are less than 9 circles in the grid, that means there is at least one marked 2x2 block which contains two pairs of touching digits.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Digits in the first column say in which column you can find number 1 in the respective row. Digits in the fifth column say in which column you can find number 5. Digits in the nineth column say in which column you can find number 9.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The set of given digits on each grey line must appear on the same line in the solution. They may or may not appear in the same order.
Follow classic sudoku rules. There is a set of 16 different two-digit numbers in the marked cages. Their order from the lowest to the highest is given by small numbers from 1 to 16.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Let’s denote D a number in the marked cell. Let’s denote Aand B the two neighbouring numbers in the cells marked by arrows. The distance between numbers A and B in an ortogonal row / column (marked by the short grey line) should be equal to D. (It is possible to have two signs in one cell.)

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid appears within the first box in the corresponding direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid appears within the first box in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter the three unordered clues in the three margin cells for that direction.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid tells the count of consecutive adjacent pairs in the particular row / column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The signs outside the grid indicate how many inequality signs should be placed between neighbouring cells in the corresponding direction.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, the clues outside the grid indicate the sum of at least one pair of neighboring cells in that row or column.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number 'N' outside the grid indicates that the first N digits from the corresponding direction have the same parity and the digit in the 'N+1'th cell has the opposite parity.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number 'N' outside the grid indicates that the first N digits from the corresponding direction have the same parity and the digit in the 'N+1'th cell has the opposite parity.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit outside the grid appears in one of 2nd, 3rd or 4th cells in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter the three unordered clues in the three margin cells for that direction.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Additionally two grids are overlapping.
Place the digits from 1 to 9 in every row and column of both overlapping grids and in every bold outlined area for the puzzle as a whole.

Shade some cells such that the numbers outside the grid indicate the number of shaded cells in each shaded stretch in the corresponding row or column. If there is more than one number, the given order is valid and there must be at least one white cell between the stretches of shaded cells. Then transfer the digits from the white cells into the blank grid and solve it as classic Sudoku. Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are a palindrome, they read the same from both directions.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are a palindrome, they read the same from both directions.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
The numbers placed in each shaded region must form the given equations when they are read as the corresponding n-digit numbers.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits along any grey line have the same parity (all odd or all even).

Follow classic sudoku rules. If there is an odd number O in a circle, there are exactly O odd numbers around it. If there is an even number E in a circle, there are exactly E even numbers around it. All possible circles are given.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are either all odd or all even.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are either all odd or all even.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits up to and including the first even digit or up to and including the first odd digit from the corresponding direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits up to and including the first even digit or up to and including the first odd digit from the corresponding direction.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each digit represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate whether an odd (O) or even (E) number of buildings is visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some cells belong to multiple rows and/or columns.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digit(s) in the respective row or column, when the row or column is partitioned at certain points (not given), in the correct order. Clues are either given for the entire row or column, or not given at all.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells with pencilmark digits must contain one of those digits.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells with pencilmark digits must contain one of those digits.

instructions: Toggle every allowed digit in the marked cell.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells, reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, that represent a two-digit perfect square are marked in the grid. All such perfect squares are marked. The list of perfect squares is 16, 25, 36, 49, 64 and 81.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells, reading left-to-right or top-to-bottom, that represent a two-digit perfect square are marked in the grid. All such perfect squares are marked. The list of perfect squares is 16, 25, 36, 49, 64 and 81.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Exactly one of the highlighted digits is a pinnochio and is incorrect.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Exactly one of the highlighted digits is a pinnochio and is incorrect.

instructions: Enter every highlighted candidate digit directly in its cell.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Place the given fragments inside the grid without rotating or mirroring. Fragments can touch each other, but can not overlap each other or given digits.

Follow irregular sudoku rules (digits 1 to 7). No digit can be repeated in a region with a dashed border. The sum of all digits in every region with a dashed border is given while digits in grey cells are counted as negative ones.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Cell with a horizontal line contains a sum of the two neighbouring cells in the particular row. Cell with a vertical line contains a difference of the two neighbouring cells in the particular column. All possible lines are given.

Apply classic sudoku rules. A grey horizontal or vertical bar symbol in a cell indicates that the digit in the cell is equal to the sum or difference of its horizontal or vertical neighbours. Not all possible bars are marked; cells without a bar may or may not indicate either sum or difference of its horizontal or vertical neighbours.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. If digit 'X' is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit 'X+1' must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. If digit 'X' is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit 'X+1' must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. If digit ‘X’ is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit ‘X-1’ must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. If digit ‘X’ is placed in a cell with an arrow, digit ‘X-1’ must be placed in one of the cells pointed by the arrow.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid indicate the number of different numbers placed in the cells in the corresponding diagonal direction.

Apply classic sudoku rules. A digit N in a cell with an arrow sees another digit N, which is N cells away in the arrow's direction (diagonally). Arrows may be reciprocal (pointing towards each other). Not all possible arrows are given.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Numbers outside the grid signify the position of the highest of the nearest three digits in the corresponding row/column.

Apply classic sudoku rule. External clues give information relating to the first two digits (called A and B) in the corresponding row or column. Clues next to the grid give the sum of A and B. Clues in the grey band further outside the grid give the sum of the digits in positions A and B.
Place digits from 1 to 9, they don't repeat in marked triangles and rows of cells in three principal directions (of any length, e.g.4+5).
Apply classic sudoku rules. Horizontally and vertically adjacent cells containing digits whose sum is a prime number are marked with a diamond. All possible diamonds are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the product of digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the product of digits in those two cells.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, some arrows are in the grid. The encircled 1, 2, or more digits must be equal to the product of the digits that appear along the path of the arrow. Digits can repeat within an arrow shape.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the product of the first three digits in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter each product in the outside margin and place an arrow in the same cell to set its direction.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the product of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The number at the top-left corner of each cage is the product of digits inside the cage. Digits do not repeat within a cage.

instructions: Draw each cage, then enter its product in the cage corner.

rules.9x9: Follow classic sudoku rules. Number in a circle is equal to the last digit of a product of all digits along the corresponding arrow.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the product of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the product of digits in the direction pointed by its arrow.

instructions: Place the product and its arrow on the same outside cell.

rules.9x9:
Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, there are some pyramids in the grid made out of grey cells. For each row above the base of the pyramid, each grey cell must equal either the sum or the difference of the two grey cells diagonally beneath it.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digit pointed by each arrow must be larger than the other three digits that the arrow touches.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digit pointed by each arrow must be larger than the other three digits that the arrow touches.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digit pointed by each arrow must be smaller than the other three digits that the arrow touches.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digit pointed by each arrow must be smaller than the other three digits that the arrow touches.

Apply classic sudoku rules. A circle at a corner implies that one digit is the sum of the remaining three digits at that 2x2 square. Not all possible dots are given, i.e. there may or may not be additional 2x2 squares where one digit is the sum of the remaining three digits.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. No 2x2 area contains four digits of the same parity.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. No 2x2 area contains four digits of the same parity.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The four digits at the intersection of four cells must be present in those four cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The four digits at the intersection of four cells must be present in those four cells.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digit 6 must not be repeated in the same diagonal of any length.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digit 9 must not be repeated in the same diagonal of any length.
Apply classic sudoku rules. A digit 'X' in a circle means that the digit in the cell is the X-th smallest number in the corresponding cage. Digits cannot repeat within a cage.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The digits placed in adjacent cells must satisfy the given ratios.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Within each 3x3 region, exactly one of two constraints (A or B) applies:
(A) Digits places in adjacent cells within the 3x3 region must not be consecutive,
(B) Each cell must be adjacent to at least one other cell within the 3x3 region where the digits places in both are consecutive.
Neither constraint applies to adjacent cells in different 3x3 regions.

Apply classic sudoku rules. For each line, digits on the line have an equal sum N within each box it passes through. If a line passes through the same box more than once, each individual segment of such a line within that box sums to N separately.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Lines in a cell mark that in those two directions both digits are the same at a distance of N where N is the value of that cell. Not all possible lines are given.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each marked line contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a line.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each marked line contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a line.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each marked region contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a region.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each marked region contains a set of consecutive digits. Digits do not repeat within a region.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each outlined cage must contain a set of distinct digits with the specified digit sum. All remaining squares form contiguous blocks of shaded cells and each block must contain a set of consecutive digits.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, all cells having the same digit(s) more than once as orthogonal neighbors are shaded.
(If a cell is not shaded, it cannot have any digits repeated as orthogonal neighbors.)

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Instead of clues for the digits 1 to 9, this sudoku gives clues for Roman numerals I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX. Not all clues are complete numbers; some of them may be just part of the Roman numeral.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each arrow outside the grid indicates that the digits in the first three cells in the corresponding direction are in ascending order in the direction of the arrow. All such arrows are marked.

instructions: Place cardinal arrows in the margin. Diagonal arrows are unavailable.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Each cage clue indicates the value of rounding off the two-digit number within that cage to the nearest multiple of 10. Numbers ending in digits 1-4 are rounded down, while numbers ending in digits 5-9 are rounded up.
The sum of four cells adjacent to a grey one is the same in the whole grid.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits between 1 and 6 in the corresponding row or column.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits between 1 and 9 in the corresponding row or column.

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column, outlined region and the set of shaded cells.

Place the digits from 1 to 9 in every row, column, bold outlined area and the nine grey cells. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the first X digits in the corresponding direction. X is the first digit in the corresponding direction.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each arrow points to at least one 6 in the corresponding direction. Each digit in a cell with an arrow is the distance from that cell to the nearest cell containing a 6.

Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each arrow points to at least one 6 in the corresponding direction. Each digit in a cell with an arrow is the distance from that cell to the nearest cell containing a 6.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each arrow points to at least one 9 in the corresponding direction. Each digit in a cell with an arrow is the distance from that cell to the nearest cell containing a 9.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Some arrows are marked in the grid. Each arrow points to the digit 9 in the respective row, column or diagonal. The digit in the cell with the arrow is the distance from this cell to the corresponding digit 9. For each diagonally oriented arrow, the digit in the cell with the arrow shows the distance to the closest digit 9 in that direction.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Some arrows are marked in the grid. Each arrow points to the digit 9 in the respective row or column. The digit in the cell with the arrow is the distance from this cell to the corresponding digit 9.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are in arithmetic progression.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each line are in arithmetic progression.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The digits along each marked line have to be different and must form an arithmetic sequence in the correct order. Two straight lines may cross each other.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the rows is the sum of digits in the 2nd and 3rd cells in the corresponding direction. Each number outside the columns is the sum of digits in the 3rd and 4th cells in the corresponding direction.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly once in every row and column. In bold regions of size N ≤ 9, the digits 1 to N must appear exactly once. In bold regions of size N > 9, the (N-9) largest digits will appear twice while the remaining digits will appear once. For example, a region with 11 cells will contain the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8, 9, 9, with the two largest digits, 8 and 9, repeated.

Blacken exactly two cells in every row, column and outlined box. Fill in the remaining blank cells with digits from a given set so that no digit is repeated in a row, column or outlined box. The sum of all digits between the two black cells is given for some rows and columns.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each shape is cloned, including the digits it contains. Position of digits within the shape may change. A cloned shape may undergo rotation and/or reflection. Digits may repeat in a shape.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Each shaded shape below the grid must replace one of the shaded regions in the grid. Shapes can be rotated, but cannot be reflected.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each digit represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate either the number of even-heighted buildings or number of odd-heighted buildings visible from the corresponding direction, or both. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit inside the grid represents the height of a skyscraper in that cell. Each number outside the grid indicates the number of skyscrapers that can be seen in the corresponding row or column. Taller skyscrapers hide shorter ones.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit inside the grid represents the height of a skyscraper in that cell. Each number outside the grid indicates the number of skyscrapers that can be seen in the corresponding row or column. Taller skyscrapers hide shorter ones.

Follow classic sudoku rules. Digits along the arrows represent buildings of the height given by its value. Number in a circle tells you the count of visible buildings along the corresponding arrow, in the direction from the circle to the opposite end of the arrow. Higher building covers all smaller or equal building behind it.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, in each row the cells between two poles must contain a set of consecutive digits.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digits in each marked row or column are in exactly the same cyclic sequence wrapped around the grid.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digits in each marked row or column are in exactly the same cyclic sequence wrapped around the grid.

instructions: Toggle columns in the Input Modes section; the editor paints each selected column.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. One or more loops of orthogonally connected cells will be shaded grey. Along such a loop, each pair of adjacent digits must have a difference of either 1 or 2.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the smallest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each arrow(s) in a cell points to the smallest digit among the digits in its adjacent cells.

instructions: Select every arrow pointing to a smallest neighbour.

rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1-6 in each empty cell so that each digit appears exactly once in each “row“ and outlined hexagon.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The sum of digits in every cage is the same throughout the grid. It is part of solving to determine the sum.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The sum of digits in every cage is the same throughout the grid. It is part of solving to determine the sum.

instructions: Draw every equal-sum cage; no cage total is entered.

rules.9x9:
Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, in each cage, at least one cell has the indicated digit.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Additionally, exactly three of the nine 3x3 boxes have the property that their eight perimeter cells are in a strictly increasing order with starting cell and direction unspecified. The 3 sprial boxes, and the starting cell/direction in these boxes need to be identified as part of solving.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digits inside each square wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Square wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digits inside each square wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Square wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, the clues outside the grid indicate the product of the digits placed in the cells containing stars in the corresponding row or column.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There is exactly one way how to go in a cycle through all grey arrows, every time making that number of steps to the next arrow which is given by the digit in the previous cell with an arrow

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each thermometer are non-decreasing from its bulb to each of its ends.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each thermometer are non-decreasing from its bulb to each of its ends.

instructions: Draw each branched or straight non-decreasing thermometer from its bulb.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. For every pair of connected cages, the set of digits in the smaller cage must be a subset of the set of digits in the larger cage. Digits on the corresponding connecting line must belong to the set of digits in the larger cage but must not belong to the subset of digits in the smaller cage. Digits are not allowed to repeat in any cage.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. A cell with an alphabet contains a digit whose mapped word contains the corresponding alphabet.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. A cell with an alphabet contains a digit whose mapped word contains the corresponding alphabet.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Some rows and columns are bent, marked by curved lines.

Apply classic sudoku rules. If any operation (+, -, x, /) performed between two orthogonally adjacent cells leads to a result of N, then the two cells are separated by a grey circle. If there is no grey circle, the result can not be N. The number N can be any integer in the range from 1-9. N is not given. It is part of solving to determine N.

Place either a digit from 1 to 7 or a star in each empty cell, so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. There must be exactly two stars in in each row, column and outlined region. Stars cannot be placed in adjacent cells that touch even at a corner.

Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each of the empty cells so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and irregular shaped outlined box. The sum of digits in each horizontal and vertical group of cells is given on its left and top respectively.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum of digits in those two cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit between adjacent cells is the sum of digits in those two cells.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The sum of the numbers placed in corresponding cells in two regions of the same shape must equal 10.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The sum of numbers in the shaded cells in each row is exactly 100. Two adjacent shaded cells in the same row form a two-digit number.

Apply classic sudoku rules. There are 22 outlined cages in the grid that have sums between 5 and 15. Every sum is used exactly twice and the combinations of digits never repeat. Some of the sums are already given. Digits may repeat in a cage.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first few digits from the edge. The nearest shaded cell in the corresponding direction indicates the number of digits included in the sum.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each arrow in the grid indicates that the sum of the first ‘n’ digits along the direction pointed by the arrow is equal to the digit in the cell for some value of ‘n’. All arrows are not marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each arrow in the grid indicates that the sum of the first ‘n’ digits along the direction pointed by the arrow is equal to the digit in the cell for some value of ‘n’. All arrows are not marked.

instructions: At each origin, toggle every direction containing the required prefix sum.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits within the first box in the corresponding direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of digits within the first box in the corresponding direction.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.9x9: Follow diagonal sudoku rules. Each row and both diagonals can be interpreted as a sequence of three numbers, a 4-digit, 3-digit (in grey cells) and 2-digit one. The total of those numbers is given.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the immediate neighbours of the digit 9 in the corresponding row or column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Adjacent cells containing numbers with the sum 9 are marked with a rhombus. Adjacent cells with no marking must not contain numbers with the sum 9.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The number in each cage shows either the sum or the product (or both) of all digits inside the cage. Digits may be repeated within a cage.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Additionally, the clues outside the grid indicate all numbers placed in the corresponding direction that are the sum of the numbers placed in the two neighbouring cells in that direction, e.g. 132. If a number is not clued outside the grid, then it must not be the sum of its two neighbours in that direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. The sum of the digits in each marked cage must be different.

Place a number from 1-9 in each empty cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked 3×3 box contains each number exactly once. Each number represents the height of a building. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of buildings visible from the corresponding direction. A taller building will hide any shorter buildings behind it.

Follow classic sudoku rules. There is one circled number N in every box. If any number of that particular box has a neighbour sharing an edge which is differing exactly by N there is a dot between them. (All possible dots are marked.)

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 4 at least once.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 5 at least once.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 6 at least once.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 7 at least once.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 8 at least once.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row and column. Each outlined region of more than one cell contains digits from 1 to 9 at least once.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The contents of any two cells in symmetrical positions relative to the centre of the grid must sum to 10. Digits placed in cells connected by a chess Knight's move must be different.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each rectangular region is of size 2xn and the number in the circle is the sum of the products of all 180-degree symmetric pairs of digits within the region. Digits may be repeated within a rectangular region.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells that are 180° symmetric about the centre must not contain the same digit.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells that are 180° symmetric about the centre must not contain the same digit.

Apply class sudoku rules. Within cages, digits must increase from top to bottom along columns and increase along rows from left to right, even if there is a gap between two cells in a cage. Digits within a cage must be distinct.

Apply Classic Sudoku rules.
Additionally, if a gray bar is given between two adjacent cells, then the two digits in those cells sum to ten or eleven. If a gray bar is not given, the two digits cannot sum to ten or eleven.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Number between a pair of adjacent cell indicates the number of the tens position of the product of the numbers placed in these cells (e.g. if 3 and 4 are placed in the cells, their product is 12 and the clue would be 1).

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Digits along each thermometer are strictly increasing from its bulb to each of its ends.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Digits along each thermometer are strictly increasing from its bulb to each of its ends.

Apply classic sudoku rules. In each group of three lines:
- One will follow Arrow Sudoku rules. The digit placed in a cell with a circle must be the sum of the digits placed in the cells that the adjoining arrow passes through. Digits may repeat on arrows. It is up to the solver to determine which end has the circle.
- One will follow Thermo Sudoku rules. Starting at the “bulb”, digits placed along the thermometer must form a strictly increasing sequence. It is up to the solver to determine which end has the bulb.
- One will follow Palindrome Sudoku rules. Digits along the line read the same in both directions.
It is allowed that a line has properties of both Arrow and Thermo Sudoku (for instance: 1-2-3-6).

Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits in the gray strings represent three-digit numbers. All three-digit numbers in the cage must sum to the total given in its top-left corner. Digits must not repeat in cages.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The central 3x3 region serves as a map to the 9 regions of the grid (for the example, its top-left cell corresponds to the top-left 3x3 region, and its central cell corresponds to the central 3x3 region). Wherever the central region contains an odd digit, the corresponding region must have three odd digits in at least one line (horizontal, vertical or diagonal). Wherever it contains an even digit, the corresponding region must have three even digits in a line. No region can contain both a line of odd digits and a line of even digits.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each 3x3 box is also a grid for a Tic-Tac-Toe game. A game is won by placing three odd digits or three even digits in a horizontal, vertical or diagonal line. Each 3x3 box contains exactly one winning combination and this combination is marked by a grey line.

rules.6x6: Place a digit (or two digits in split cells) from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. In each cell split into halves, the smaller digit must be above the larger digit.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. Each marked line contains exactly one identical digit.
Apply classic sudoku rules. For every pair of vertically adjacent digits where both digits lie in the range 1-7, the upper digit must be greater than the lower digit.

rules.4x4: Place a digit from 1 to 4 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.
rules.5x5: Place a digit from 1 to 5 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.
rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.
rules.7x7: Place a digit from 1 to 7 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.
rules.8x8: Place a digit from 1 to 8 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and outlined region. Some outlined regions wrap around the grid.

Complete the grid so that each row, column and 3x3 box contains 8 distinct digits in the range 1-9. The digits in the cells orthogonally adjacent to each black square must give an identical total sum.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each digit has at least one consecutive digit in its adjacent cells.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each digit has at least one consecutive digit in its adjacent cells.

The digits in the gray cells have been erased from a filled Classic Sudoku grid. Then four digits in every row are transferred to the right one after another. Also, four digits in every column are transferred down one after another. Each digit that was not erased was moved outside the grid to either a row or column clue, but not both. Restore all digits to the grid to reform the starting Sudoku.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Cells with circles contain digits 1, 2 or 3. Cells with squares contain digits 4, 5 or 6.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Cells with circles contain digits 1, 2 or 3. Cells with squares contain digits 4, 5 or 6. Cells with triangles contain digits 7, 8 or 9.

Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell or blacken the cell so that each digit appears exactly once in every row, column, and outlined 3x3 region along with three black cells. Each black cell should be part of an orthogonally connected group of three blackened cells (a triomino). No two triominoes can share an edge.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Each row is divided into 3 parts. The 1st part is a 4-digit number, the 2nd part (shaded) is a 3-digit number and the 3rd part is a 2-digit number. The sum of these three numbers is given outside the grid in the corresponding row. Not all outside clues may be given.

Apply classic sudoku rules. Wherever the number in the cell equals to a total of any amount of the closest numbers in any direction, there is an arrow pointing to that direction possible arrows are given).
Apply classic sudoku rules. Adjacent cells in the indicated direction (left-to-right or top-to-bottom) containing a two-digit prime number are marked with a diamond. All possible diamonds are marked.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Chess Knights, placed in cells with number 9, must attack only different numbers.

variants.otherNames: Ordered sums Sudoku, Unique Order Sums Sudoku
variants.name: Unique Ordered Sums

rules.9x9:
Apply classic sudoku rules. There are numbered cages in the grid. All cages are ordered according to the sum of digits in the cage. A cage marked with number 1 has the lowest sum, a cage with number 2 has a higher sum than a cage marked with number 1 … and so on.
It is possible for two or more cages marked with the same number to appear in the grid. That means they have the same sum (not necessary the same digits).
Follow classic sudoku rules. If any four cells in the grid lays in exactly two rows and two columns, there should be more than two different digits in them.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Shaded regions must contain a set of distinct, consecutive digits in any order. Every renban group consists of an unique set of digits.

Place a number from the range 1-X in each unnumbered cell in the grid such that each row, column and marked box contains each number from the range 1-X exactly once, where X is the number of cells in each row.
Several "thermometers" in the grid each indicate a sequence of cells. The numbers in a thermometer must be strictly increasing (no duplicates) from one end of the thermometer to the other. You are not told which end is which.
The digits along a grey line goes alternately up and down while the difference between the two neighbouring digits is at least 4.

Apply classic sudoku rules. A digit N in a grey cell represents a watchtower, that can overlook exactly N cells, including itself, with digits smaller than N in all four directions up to the first larger digit in that direction. All possible watchtowers are given.

Apply classic sudoku rules. The clues outside the grid indicate the sum of the digits contained in the cells in the direction of the corresponding arrow. Digits in gray cells have to be counted twice. Digits may repeat within a sum.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. The digits inside each wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. The digits inside each wheel must be located in those four cells in the given order. Wheels can be rotated, but not reflected.

instructions: Place four clue digits and a large circle at the same four-cell intersection.

rules.9x9: Apply classic sudoku rules. The digits on the circles have to be placed in the same circular order in the four cells that are touched by the circle. The circles may have to be rotated to the correct position by 90, 180 or 270 degrees.
Apply classic sudoku rules. One wildcard. Cells with '>' contain digits greater than wildcard. Cells with '<' contain digits smaller than wildcard.
Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each marked 3x3 region contains each digit from 1 to 9.

instructions: On a 9 × 9 grid, four 3 × 3 cages are created automatically at r2c2, r2c5, r5c2, and r5c5.

rules.9x9: Place a symbol from the given set into each of the empty cells so that each symbol appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each given word must appear horizontally, vertically, or diagonally - forward or backward, in the grid.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
The clues outside the grid differ by exactly 1 from the sum of the first 3 numbers placed in the corresponding direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Clues outside the grid indicate the exact average of the first X digits seen from that side, where X is the first digit from that side.

Apply classic sudoku rules.
The number placed in a circled cell must appear exactly once on each of the two marked diagonals passing through that cell.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X numbers placed in the corresponding direction, where X is the first digit placed in that direction.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Each number outside the grid is the sum of the first X numbers placed in the corresponding direction, where X is the first digit placed in that direction.

instructions: Enter each clue in the outside margin.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 6 are marked by VI. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 11 are marked by XI. All possible VI and XI are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 6 are marked by VI. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 11 are marked by XI. All possible VI and XI are marked.

instructions: Place VI on every adjacent pair summing to 6 and XI on every pair summing to 11.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 5 are marked by V. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 10 are marked by X. All possible V and X are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 5 are marked by V. Adjacent cells with digits summing to 10 are marked by X. All possible V and X are marked.

rules.6x6: Place a digit from 1 to 6 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 2x3 outlined box. Horizontally adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding row. Vertically adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding column. All such circles (or diamonds) are marked.
rules.9x9: Place a digit from 1 to 9 into each empty cell in the grid so that each digit appears exactly once in each row, column and 3x3 outlined box. Horizontally adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding row. Vertically adjacent cells marked by a circle (or diamond) contain digits whose absolute difference is equal to the first digit of the corresponding column. All such circles (or diamonds) are marked.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Digits in the top-left corner of a marked cage must all appear in the cage. Digits may repeat in cages.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Equal digits may not be a chess king move apart.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the sum of the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th cells in that row or column in that direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the digit that is missing from the first three cells in that row or column in that direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the sum of the immediate neighbors of the digit 9 in that row or column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the count of consecutive adjacent pairs in that row or column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the count of neighboring cells in that row or column that contain a digit greater than their neighbor in that direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the sum of at least one pair of neighboring cells in that row or column.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate whether an odd or even number of skyscrapers are visible in that direction.
Apply classic sudoku rules. Outside clues indicate the count of distinct digits along the diagonal starting in that direction.
